ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers French toast as a bread-based egg-dipped fried dish; cinnamon variant is a flavour modification of the same preparation class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP maps to French toast; cinnamon flavouring is a preparation modifier not requiring a separate base code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for French toast or cinnamon French toast identified in the clinical terminology hierarchy; ontology anchor satisfied by FoodOn and FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cinnamon French toast in Singapore is approximately 2 slices (about 120 g), pan-fried and served warm, sometimes with a light dusting of cinnamon sugar or a drizzle of condensed milk.

nutrient_source.title: French Toast — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists French toast (cinnamon variant) with per-100g values matching the input record exactly. All twelve nutrient fields in the input are sourced from HPB at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for French toast (NDB 18033) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; see notes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for cinnamon French toast in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is assigned based on the closest analogous entry: white-bread-based French toast (GI ~65, medium category) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Cinnamon has evidence of modest GI-lowering effect but insufficient to shift category; GI retained at 65. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 48.7) / 100 ≈ 31.7 for a 120 g serving (48.74 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 535 mg/100 g combined with sugar 12.86 g/100 g and low dietary fibre 1.3 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid cinnamon French toast due to its high sodium content (535 mg/100 g) and elevated sugar load (12.86 g/100 g with fibre only 1.3 g/100 g), which together promote sodium retention and vascular stress; if consumed, request preparation with unsalted butter, no added salt, and avoid sweet toppings such as maple syrup or condensed milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~31.7 per serving), refined white bread base, sugar 12.86 g/100 g, and low dietary fibre 1.3 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cinnamon French toast is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high glycemic load (~31.7 per 120 g serving), refined white bread base, and added sugar (12.86 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (1.3 g/100 g), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; substituting wholegrain or sourdough bread, omitting sweet toppings, and significantly reducing portion size are essential modifications if this food is to be consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~2.8 g/100 g, trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g, sodium 535 mg/100 g, and cholesterol 20 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cinnamon French toast poses a moderate cardiovascular risk due to its saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g from butter), trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and high sodium (535 mg/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request preparation with olive oil instead of butter, and avoid added sugar toppings to reduce atherogenic and hypertensive load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate inflammatory load, saturated fat from butter, trace trans fat, and high sugar 12.86 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cinnamon French toast made with white bread and butter contributes refined carbohydrate and saturated fat that promote pro-inflammatory mediators; substituting wholegrain bread and olive oil, retaining cinnamon for its anti-inflammatory properties, and avoiding sweet toppings are recommended modifications for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cinnamon French Toast is a pan-fried bread-based dish popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, prepared by dipping slices of white bread in a seasoned egg-and-milk batter flavoured with ground cinnamon, then frying in butter until golden brown on both sides. The result is a warm, lightly crisp exterior with a soft, custardy interior carrying a sweet, spiced aroma. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a breakfast or snack item, sometimes accompanied by condensed milk, maple syrup, or a dusting of cinnamon sugar, and is found in kopitiams, cafes, and home kitchens. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 278 kcal, 7.52 g protein, 9.18 g fat (approximately 2.8 g saturated), 40.62 g carbohydrate, 12.86 g sugar, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 535 mg sodium. The high sodium content and elevated sugar with low fibre make it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or type 2 diabetes without significant modification. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium category) based on its white bread base, with a glycemic load of approximately 31.7 per standard 120 g serving. Cinnamon itself provides modest anti-inflammatory and hypoglycaemic properties but is insufficient to offset the refined carbohydrate and sodium burden of the dish. Wholegrain bread substitution and elimination of sweet toppings are the primary recommended modifications for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is French toast suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat French toast with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all twelve reported fields. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (French toast, NDB 18033) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; these are marked as adapted. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed source. Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source coverage of all core macros.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (3.5 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.9 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for French toast (NDB 18033) as HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item; values are adapted proportionally to match the HPB total fat of 9.18 g/100 g. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific cinnamon French toast preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the level of this dish variant as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white-bread French toast from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); cinnamon's modest GI-lowering effect is acknowledged but insufficient to shift the category. 4. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Malay' and occasion_tags as 'lunch' and 'dinner'; these have been reviewed — French toast is more accurately classified as a Singaporean/Malay-influenced Western dish consumed primarily at breakfast and brunch; meal_context has been updated accordingly. 5. The promotional prefix 'Cozy' in the original name 'Cozy Cinnamon French Toast' has been removed from the canonical food_name per Q1 requirements; it is retained as an alias.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed promotional prefix 'Cozy' from canonical food_name; retained as alias.
Added all five mandatory Singaporean-dish language names: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Added saturated_fat_g (2.8), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.5), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.9), trans_fat_g (0.1) estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 18033 and documented in notes.
Added GI (65, medium) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 with full gi_source documentation.
Added glycemic_load_per_serving (31.7) calculated for 120 g serving.
Replaced input health_suitability conditions with RESULT-template-compliant health_context including Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (correct key and human_condition_name), and Cardiovascular Disease.
Documented all -1 fields in notes per g3 requirement.
Added comprehensive llm_training section with 200+ character description, user queries, meal context, and ingredient synonyms.
Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with honest gap notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency (q8 self-check passed).
Updated meal_context from lunch/dinner to breakfast/brunch/snack to reflect accurate consumption context for French toast.
